channel.title: Comments for page "Image Use Policy"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "Image Use Policy"

item.title: Licensing Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
On the EN wiki, we allow it on the basis that EN plans to forward port from BY-SA 3.0 to BY-SA 4.0 on an unspecified date in the future.

Other wikis may have a more strict view of the licensing policy. In that case, you would want to check with them to see what their specific rules are when dealing with 4.0 images should you plan on posting there.

Be advised if your article survives and gets translated, wikis that prohibit 4.0 images will not be including the image with the translation.

item.title: Is CC BY 4.0 allowed?
item.content:encoded: I found an image recently (just a few minutes ago, actually), that seems to me like a great basis for an SCP article. It's licensed under CC BY 4.0. I think that that's allowed, based on this policy page, but I'm not quite sure. (CC BY 4.0 isn't on the list of allowed licenses, but the list of allowed licenses is immediately followed by a paragraph explaining that CC BY 4.0 is disallowed on some international versions of the wiki but not the English one.) Is this license allowed or not? Thank you! :)

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The Image Use Policy has been rewritten! Please view this rewrite if you haven't already.

item.title: Non-Copyright Images (CC0 1.0)
item.content:encoded:
Are the rules different around modifying an image if the image is non-copyrighted? I'll naturally still credit, as the rules state, but if I wanted to crop down a non-copyright image I found through CC, what is the wiki's stance on it?
(For reference, this is what CC has to say on the matter; [https://creativecommons.org/publicdomain/zero/1.0/])

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think it's about time the SCP Foundation adopted CC BY-SA 4.0.

Recently, CC BY-SA 4.0 images have increased. The licensing team at the SCP Foundation allows CC BY-SA 4.0 images, which is a bit odd. BY-SA licenses can be inherited in future versions, but not vice versa.

Indeed, if an article and an image are considered independent works, then it doesn't matter if CC BY-SA 4.0 is used in the article, in terms of the CC license. By this logic, you should be able to use BY-ND images, but I'm not going to argue that far this time.

But this is a wiki. Users are free to write articles, and they can also write articles based on BY-SA 4.0 images. In that case, the article would have to inherit BY-SA 4.0 because it's a derivative of the image, and the SCP Foundation is absolutely BY-SA 3.0, so there could be a license violation. The following links will be helpful in thinking about this:
Can I reuse an excerpt of a larger work that is licensed with the NoDerivs restriction? (Frequently Asked Questions - Creative Commons)

The solution to this is simple, just have the SCP Foundation update to BY-SA 4.0. Fortunately, you can set any license for the Wikidot footer.

Admin panel -> General Settings -> License -> Choose ‘Other’ -> Edit ‘Custom license'

Example:

Unless otherwise stated, the content of this page is licensed under <a rel="license" href="https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/">Creative Commons Attribution-ShareAlike 4.0 License</a>

I'd appreciate it if you would consider it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
And I'm floored that someone who thinks themselves savvy enough to do business online couldn't figure out how to contact our powers that be.

Well… keep in mind that the Dragonsnails case was years and years ago, before the current site layout and navigation setup. If I recall correctly, the "Contact Staff" page didn't exist back when the artist was initially approached.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well that's that, reality beats theory every time.
Consider all my concerns and questions raised above moot and/or void (since I can never remember which state applies to what type of thing).

And I'm floored that someone who thinks themselves savvy enough to do business online couldn't figure out how to contact our powers that be.

But then again, I know a fellow who can make welded steel look like a living tree but has literally put himself in the hospital trying to make a cup of tea. Skill in one area doesn't necessarily imply general capability.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On the other hand, those pics have been publicly posted on the wiki for a decade or more with no claimants coming forward. It is overwhelmingly likely that there are either no copyright issues or the rights-holder doesn't care.

We can't be sure. As was the case with 173, and the Dragonsnails article, and the Mewts, SCP wiki staff had to reach out to the creators to confirm they were okay with the creative commons licensing… and none of them were okay with just releasing the images. In the case of the Dragonsnails image owner, they were actually extremely upset with the wiki's usage of the picture, but couldn't act on it because they didn't know how to contact the site staff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh, yeah — I didn't mean to imply you were maligning anyone (I think you've been very respectful and straightforward with your criticisms!) — I just thought it was something important to bring up.

I don't have any idea about the background or credentials of the licensing team; I only meant that the current policy is a product of over a decade of experience dealing with this issue (including things like what happened with SCP-173). While I would expect legal experts to easily drive circles around us both on this matter, I also think the policy reflects that history, and represents lessons learned from the whole SCP-173 debacle (along with numerous other cases).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fair, all fair.
And I don't mean to malign the effort staff put in, any implication to the contrary was strictly unintentional.
Lord knows I wouldn't have the patience or stamina shown by any member of the team on a daily basis!

But you just mentioned something that I want to pin down precisely.

…a lot of people with a lot of knowledge have put a lot of time and effort into working out the wiki's current policy.

If the current image policy was hammered out by folks who have actual knowledge of copyright law, then I'm out of my rhetorical weight class and happily yield to wiser heads. With renewed faith in humanity even!

But all the discussion I've seen so far from staff has gone out of the way to point out that they don't have credentials in the subject.

That's actually why I felt the need to chime in now, I saw a discussion page that made it sound like my own strictly passive experience with copyright claims might be the closest thing we have to hard data.1

Footnotes
1. Plus in all honesty, a little personal investment after the image policy impacted my first skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-173 is a great case-study, because it really demonstrates a situation where just about no one is genuinely happy with the end result.

Authors don't want to add these captions to their articles (it's both an invitation to find the original copyright owner and a tacit admission of guilt: "I couldn't figure out where this image came from, so I decided to use it anyway"). Artists don't want people hunting them down to bug them about using their work in new projects (notice the SCP-173 caption tells people to stop contacting the artist? There's a reason for that!). And readers don't want the disruption of being reminded that these images are potential copyright infringements.

I get what you're saying, but this is a collaborative project — and part of being a collaborative project means taking responsibility for what you contribute to it. Cite your sources. If you can't cite them? Don't use them.

One more thing to consider: While I think it's fine and good that we're having this conversation, remember that a lot of people with a lot of knowledge have put a lot of time and effort into working out the wiki's current policy. Challenging it like this is fine, but it's also good to remember that copyright is complicated, and this policy is built to make it as simple and harmless as possible. "Cite your sources — or don't use them" is about as simple and harmless as it gets.

ETA: And in case I haven't made this clear — the current policy is in place to avoid situations like SCP-173. We don't want an article catching fire only to discover it and all works derived from it (on the wiki or otherwise) aren't fair use. We don't want artists to feel like we've stolen their work (even if we're putting captions on them, it can still feel that way). And we don't want to propagate images that require copyright warnings ("this might not be permissible for us to use here — who knows?!") everywhere they appear (either throughout the wiki or elsewhere).

Being clear, straightforward, and unambiguous about where our images came from (and whether or not we have the right to use them) is the best way to go. Especially for a collaborative, extendable project like this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's a fair point.
The extended universe does put us in a position with more-than-legal-minimum responsibility.

But if you'll forgive me changing my position in mid-conversation; you just laid the better solution.

Either we have to show that all our content complies or warn people that it does not. Or put a caption on each article that doesn't (such as the case in SCP-173).

These three options done together would cover the wiki both legally and morally. Without the disruption of deleting images because they may, potentially, at some point in the conceivable future, cause hassle.

What we are doing now is like amputating a toe because it has a hangnail. Yes, that might lead to infection, and amputation might become necessary. But until the complication actually happens, the manifest consequences of acting now outweigh the merely potential and theoretical consequences being avoided.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The whole headache surrounding SCP-173 and its image is a consequence of this previous "innocent until proven guilty" policy. Once an article becomes so intimately tied to an image, retroactively removing that image can be incredibly disruptive not just to the article but the larger community around it.

Remember: All the derivative works (such as video games and other things that extend outside of the SCP wiki) that use our content means they're trusting us that our content is free-for-use. Putting everyone in a position where this trust could be broken — where an artist could feasibly show up ten years down the line and tell everyone involved that they've been stealing their work this whole while — is both incredibly irresponsible and just an all-around shitty thing for us to do.

"Guilty until proven innocent" isn't just a good defensive standard to protect us legally; it's morally responsible behavior. The drawback is that we have to put extra effort into securing the images that match our articles — okay, fine. But the benefit? We earn the trust both of artists who provide their work (see SCP-3199) to us and the trust of those who make derivative works that the content of our articles is, in fact, free and safe to use.

Yeah, the retroactive application sucks — but it's a necessary evil. You can't just say "okay, going forward, we require proof of compliance" and ignore all the articles that aren't compliant. Either we have to show that all our content complies or warn people that it does not. Or put a caption on each article that doesn't (such as the case in SCP-173).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A clarification: I don't think we can rely on authors to do any due diligence. The current practice of having the image team search for sources after the fact is the only way to go.

This is a perfectly understandable though process, thank you Elogee and Zyn.
It's good to know that policy is being decided with protecting the site a forethought (not that I ever doubted that was the case).

But the current abundance of caution is having some serious negative impact, out of proportion to the level of risk it is mitigating. A lot of images are being stripped, particularly from series 1 and 2 skips. Which stands to reason as they were from the truly wild-west days of the wiki before anyone gave licensing a thought.

In many cases, removing those images guts the impact of the writing. A picture is worth a thousand words and all that. Fairly often, the writing was specifically tailored to support the creepy image and just doesn't work if you remove that foundation.

On the other hand, those pics have been publicly posted on the wiki for a decade or more with no claimants coming forward. It is overwhelmingly likely that there are either no copyright issues or the rights-holder doesn't care.

And in the worst case, if a copyright holder contacted us today making a claim, all they could legally do is request that we take down the picture. Not the article; and certainly not the whole site. If we refused to remove the image, then things could get legally sticky. But as long as we take reasonably prompt action after being informed of a claim, the site is legally bulletproof.

I can only speak for US copyright law. I have no experience at all with the standards in other countries. But a copyright attorney did tell me once that the US courts are considered "hard mode" for defendants, and that he much preferred cases in the EU due to their much stronger protection for fair use and the like.

So if we CYA in the USA, we should be beyond golden internationally.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is there a reason for the switch?

Elogee provides a good explanation (from my perspective as someone who is not a lawyer and isn't familiar with copyright law), and I'd like to note a general detail as a site admin and Forum Crit captain who has seen lots of drafts over the years:

A google image search would be the minimum due diligence effort on our part.

A lot of newer authors, particularly those in the younger crowd, don't even bother doing this. Or they just briefly look at the page they got the image from, and if they don't see anything glaringly telling them they can't use the image, they'll use it anyway. We see a lot of monster images pulled from deviantart for drafts, even though dA automatically puts a copyright on every new submission, unless the artist specifies different usage permission (as is the case with stock images).

I think it's good that the Licensing Team is acting on the safe side, and essentially leading by example. It raises awareness in the community at large, and helps spread knowledge of the fact that images need to be sourced properly and authors need to know how to do so.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hello, Operational Staff for the Licensing Team and largely the driving force behind recent image enforcement efforts here:

I started my licensing staff position in February of this year, and thus I haven't looked into standards for image-sourcing in previous years terribly hard. My general impression of previous standards is that they were too lax and, at times, incorrect (such as the casual allowance of non-commercial images), which further added to my disinterest in continuing old standards. Thus, this is less of a "switch" and more of a "this was always my basis for making decisions". With no licensing staff telling me otherwise, I've always held this "guilty until proven innocent" policy and assumed that was what it always was (this had a pretty visible president in the 106 replacement contest, which was before I became staff).

In regards to the validity of the strategy you've set forth, note that I have no outside legal experience with copyright law, and so may be incorrect. But my personal policy was to always assume the worst. If I cannot source an image (and I always use a variety of reverse-image search programs to attempt to do so), I imagine the copyright holder could come to the wiki and completely disregard any opposing arguments to not only taking down the image but perhaps deleting the article or even taking down the site. Yes, this is a worst-case scenario, and most likely the copyright holder will simply request a take-down of the image if they don't want it on our site, but the off-chance that some angry copyright holder will attempt to take down the site scares me, especially if they have lawyers. We do not have lawyers.

Thus, I hold a "guilty until proven innocent" policy to make sure we aren't taking any possible risk. We may be able to defend ourselves with the reasoning you've set forth, but we don't know who we'll be dealing with.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Apologies if I'm rehashing old discussion already had elsewhere. But this recently rubbed up against my own skip, so it's new to me.

The current execution of the image policy seems to be "guilty until proven innocent".
That is, an image with no clear source is assumed to be CC-incompatible and removed.

But judging by the discussion here back in the day, there used to be explicit language saying that an image without provenance would be kept on the wiki until someone claimed it and asked for it to be taken down.

Is there a reason for the switch?

My personal experience with trademark and copyright law1 is that it is the responsibility of the owner to let their claim be publicly known.

A google image search would be the minimum due diligence effort on our part.
No relevant results found is a textbook basis for assuming no one is claiming rights, and the wiki can keep using the pic in legally bulletproof good faith.

Footnotes
1. As a rights holder attempting to enforce against profiting pirates, defending myself against false accusations of piracy, and one time where I was actually in the wrong.

item.title: Re: Images from my PC?
item.content:encoded:
The How to Write an SCP Guide has instructions on how to upload an image to your sandbox page.

If you are still having issues, please ask for assistance in the Questions Desk forum: http://www.scp-wiki.net/forum/c-891087/help:questions-desk

item.title: Images from my PC?
item.content:encoded: I have my own images in my computer, but I can't seem to be able to put it in my article. Can anyone help? Maybe a bit of code or something?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ideally people are posting images that are compliant if they intend them as fuel. In reality they aren't, but this is probably something the reddit team should be addressing. I recommend asking posters to source their images. Images are that aren't compliant shouldn't belong in the weekly fuel thread at all, and since they aren't fuel they probably are violating the "Is this an SCP?" rule or the meme rule or both. So report them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But an SCP based on the SCP Fuel post is OK if we don't use the image, right? I found some "Fuel" posts on Reddit that actually made me want to go through with that idea!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Likely not. You have to find the original source and this has to be complient.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is it OK to use an image from the official SCP Foundation subreddit as long as we cite the Redditor who posted it? The subreddit has some great SCP Fuel images!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Apologies I accidentally uploaded an image file and cant seem to take it down now :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Almost certainly yes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So what about a screenshot from a movie or a music video or something along those lines.

Does that fall under copyright?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Please ask these sorts of questions in the Questions Desk forum, where wiki syntax/coding queries are handled: http://www.scp-wiki.net/forum/c-891087/help:questions-desk

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How do I add an image in the first place?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Accidentally attached an image to this page thinking it was my SCP article. Sorry guys.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If you're looking an image for an SCP article or tale, you can make a thread asking for help in the General Advice forum.

Generally, if you're asking someone to create an image for you, it's good to at least have a draft ready so they have a. an idea of what description the image should match and b. some indication that you're committed to whatever final product will be using the image they're creating for you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is there an existing thread where we can request created images?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
They were at one point, but I'm not sure what happened to the activity regarding that project. Also, I don't think the Image Team addresses images in the newest-posted articles (notably, pages obviously in deletion range).

They might regrouping and finding volunteers though, or working through staff restructure.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So I thought the image team was removing all images from articles that they couldn't determine CC compliance for, but I guess maybe this guide is dated or something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes. It's still a good idea to double-check for permissions though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Wait, so, can we use the images from the visual records for SCPs?

item.title: Regarding free wallpapers
item.content:encoded: If I were to go to a free wallpaper website and use one of their pictures, how would that work? Are said pictures under public domin or do I have to get permission first?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, what if the image I intend to use is actually the result of manipulation/combination of several images, one of which might be copyrighted and the others are *source unknown* (mostly found via google image, specifically, hosted on a website about AIDS being a bioweapon…), would having one permission (from the one where I can sorta track down the author) be okay-dokay ?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I suppose it's possible that it's not. I don't know, this is overwhelming and confusing and more than a little aggravating. I wouldn't be nearly so angry if it weren't integral to the article itself. I took the image off 1381 anyway, because A) I got it off a furniture store's website, and B) who really cares anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If no source can be found, the image may be used until we receive notification—from the creator or another, knowledgeable source—that it is in violation of a copyright.

I looked up your picture, and it got 8 pages of results (that's a lot). I'm pretty sure you won't get sued by the picture taker, since he/she may not even be alive.

You could always look on research sites with the picture, and trace the bibliography, I guess.

And I have no idea if it is copyrighted or not, so do what you like.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mark down where you got it from in the discussion page. If you really don't want to risk it, I think the article would be fine without that picture.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
'Mokay. Thank goodness I've only used two images, and one of them I took myself. Time to go trash the other one, because I know very well where it came from.

EDIT: Oh wait, I just remembered I have five skips, not four. This is going to fuck up SCP-740 something awful. What the hell can I do about it when I need a particular image like that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know of any better way to go about it than reverse image search — if that doesn't turn up anything, you could try asking on dedicated forums for the topic at hand where it might have come from, I suppose.

The attribution can go on the article's discussion page.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh boy, copyright rears its ugly head and fucks up something else.

Any resources for finding the owners of images? I've never had any luck with reverse image searches. Also, where should that attribution thing go, in the article text? Is it code, does it show up in the article?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not necessarily. But we will be trying to source them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …so, we'll be deleting pretty much every image from every SCP then?

item.title: Public Domain + Creative Commons image resources:
item.content:encoded:
http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Wikipedia:Public_domain_image_resources has a fuckton of places to get shit from the public domain — that is, absolutely free to use for whatever you want without asking anybody for permission.

http://search.creativecommons.org/ lets you search various domains for things that fall under the creative commons license. It's super cool. Just make sure you write down who to attribute the picture to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ewwww now we have to do work for our images

But really, I'm ok with this. A Reverse image search is all you need in finding an image source.
